case_name: Grainger & Son v Gough (Surveyor of Taxes)
legal_principle: A price list or catalogue circulated by a wine merchant to customers is not an offer capable of acceptance but merely an invitation to treat. An offer to buy is made by the customer placing an order, which the merchant may accept or reject. Were the price list an offer, acceptance by every customer would bind the merchant even if stock were exhausted.
key_quote: The transmission of such a price list does not amount to an offer to supply an unlimited quantity of the wine described at the price named.
full_summary: Grainger & Son were wine merchants who circulated price lists for wine to customers in England. The question arose whether the price list constituted an offer (making acceptance by a customer a binding contract of sale) or merely an invitation to treat. The House of Lords held the price list was an invitation to treat. The merchant makes no offer by circulating a catalogue; the customer makes the offer when placing an order. This preserves freedom for the merchant to accept or decline—particularly important where stock is limited. Grainger is the authority establishing that price lists and catalogues are invitations to treat, not offers. It is closely related to Partridge v Crittenden (advertisements) and Harvey v Facey (price quotations). For FE-1, it reinforces the fundamental rule distinguishing offers from invitations to treat in commercial dealings.

legal_principle: An advertisement that goods will be sold by auction on a certain day is not an offer to sell those goods, and no binding contract arises if the auctioneer withdraws the goods before auction. The advertisement is merely a declaration of intention to hold an auction, not a contractual offer capable of acceptance.
key_quote: The advertisement of an auction sale is not an offer to sell … it is merely a declaration of intention to hold a sale.
full_summary: Nickerson advertised an auction of office furniture over three days. Harris travelled from London to the auction, only to find the relevant lots had been withdrawn. He claimed his travel expenses as damages for breach of contract. The Queen's Bench held that the auction advertisement was not an offer—it was merely a declaration of intention to hold a sale. No contract arose until a bid was made and accepted. Harris had no contractual entitlement; the auctioneer was free to withdraw lots without liability. Harris v Nickerson is the leading authority on auction advertisements and sits alongside Partridge v Crittenden (advertisements generally) and Pharmaceutical Society v Boots (shop displays) in defining the scope of invitation to treat. Important for FE-1 as it confirms that advertising an event does not create contractual obligations to proceed.

case_name: Lefkowitz v Great Minneapolis Surplus Store
legal_principle: An advertisement may constitute a binding offer capable of acceptance where it is sufficiently clear, definite, and explicit in its terms, leaving nothing open for negotiation. Where an advertisement specifies a price, quantity, and time ('first come, first served'), it may be a genuine offer that the advertiser is bound to honour upon acceptance.
key_quote: Where the offer is clear, definite and explicit, and leaves nothing open for negotiation, it constitutes an offer, acceptance of which will complete the contract.
full_summary: A Minneapolis store advertised a fur coat for $1 'first come, first served' on a specific day. Lefkowitz presented himself first and tendered $1. The store refused to sell to him, claiming the offer was 'for women only' (a rule announced after he arrived). The court held that the advertisement was a genuine offer because it was clear, definite, and left nothing open for negotiation — $1, first come, first served. The store was bound by it. Lefkowitz is the leading American case holding an advertisement to be an offer. Although the Irish and English default rule (Partridge, Harris v Nickerson) is that advertisements are invitations to treat, Lefkowitz illustrates the exception: a sufficiently specific advertisement can be an offer. Used comparatively in FE-1 to explore the boundaries of the invitation-to-treat rule.

legal_principle: A humorous television commercial depicting a product available in exchange for 'Pepsi Points' is not a unilateral offer capable of binding acceptance. Advertisements are generally invitations to treat. A reasonable person would not understand a joke advertisement (depicting a US military Harrier jet for 7 million Pepsi Points) as a genuine offer, so no contract could arise from tendering the points.
key_quote: The commercial … did not constitute an offer … no objective person could reasonably have concluded that the commercial actually offered consumers a Harrier Jet.
full_summary: Pepsico ran a television commercial as part of its 'Pepsi Stuff' promotion, humorously depicting a teenager arriving at school in a Harrier jump jet. A caption showed '7,000,000 Pepsi Points.' Leonard accumulated points and tendered them (topped up with $700,000) seeking delivery of a Harrier jet. The court held no contract existed: the commercial was an advertisement (invitation to treat, not an offer), and even if treated as an offer, no reasonable person would understand it as a genuine offer to sell a military aircraft. The objective test of offer applies — the court asks what a reasonable person in the position of the alleged offeree would have understood. Leonard v Pepsico is used in FE-1 to illustrate the objective test in offer and acceptance and the limits of treating advertisements as offers.

legal_principle: Where a local authority sends a tenant a letter of agreement containing all terms of a proposed sale and stating 'if you sign and return the agreement we will send the agreement signed on behalf of the council,' this constitutes a valid offer capable of acceptance. Once the tenant signs and returns the agreement, a binding contract is formed, even if the council has not yet countersigned.
key_quote: When the council sent the agreement signed by the town clerk and invited Mr Storer to sign, that was a definite offer … the moment he signed and returned it, there was a concluded contract.
full_summary: Storer was a council tenant who applied to purchase his house. The council sent a form headed 'Agreement for Sale' with details filled in, inviting him to sign and return it. He did so. Before the council countersigned, a change in council policy meant they no longer wished to sell. The Court of Appeal held a binding contract had been formed when Storer signed and returned the agreement. The council's letter was an offer; his signature was acceptance. Contrast with Gibson v Manchester City Council (decided the same year) where 'may be prepared to sell' language was held not to be an offer. Storer and Gibson together illustrate how the precise language of communications determines whether a contract is formed. Important for FE-1 on the offer/acceptance analysis in conveyancing and public law contexts.

legal_principle: Where two parties simultaneously send identical offers to each other ('cross-offers'), no contract is formed because acceptance must be communicated in response to an offer—an offer cannot accept another offer. Cross-offers that happen to match do not constitute agreement.
key_quote: The letters, being cross-offers, did not constitute a binding contract; neither was an acceptance of the other.
full_summary: Hoffmann wrote to Tinn offering to sell 800 tons of iron at a stated price. On the same day, without knowledge of Hoffmann's letter, Tinn wrote to Hoffmann offering to buy 800 tons at the same price. The court held no contract was formed. Although the letters crossed and were identical in terms, each was an offer, not an acceptance of the other. For a contract to form, one offer must be answered by an acceptance; cross-offers, however coincidental in their terms, do not satisfy this requirement. Tinn v Hoffmann is the authority for the cross-offers rule and is important for FE-1 in understanding the mechanics of offer and acceptance, particularly the requirement that acceptance must be in response to an offer.

case_name: Yates Building Co Ltd v RJ Pulleyn & Sons (York) Ltd
legal_principle: A stipulated method of acceptance is mandatory only if the offeror makes clear that no other method will suffice. Where the stipulated method is directory (included for the offeree's benefit or convenience, such as ensuring proof of delivery), a different method that achieves the same purpose constitutes a valid acceptance.
key_quote: The requirement of registered or recorded delivery was a directory condition, inserted for the benefit of the offeree; acceptance by ordinary post which arrived safely was equally effective.
full_summary: An option agreement required acceptance 'by notice in writing sent by registered or recorded delivery post.' The option holder sent acceptance by ordinary first-class post, which arrived on time. The grantor argued the acceptance was invalid because the stipulated method was not used. The Court of Appeal held acceptance was valid. The requirement for registered post was directory — inserted for the offeree's protection (to ensure proof of delivery) — not mandatory. Since ordinary post arrived safely within time, the purpose of the stipulated method was achieved. Acceptance was effective. Yates Building Co is an important case on method of acceptance for FE-1: it distinguishes mandatory from directory methods and shows that where a prescribed method achieves the same purpose as performance, contract is formed.

case_name: Daulia Ltd v Four Millbank Nominees Ltd
legal_principle: Once the offeree of a unilateral contract has commenced performance, the offeror cannot revoke the offer. The offer becomes irrevocable at the point when the offeree begins to perform the requested act. This implied term in unilateral contracts protects offerees who have acted in reliance on the offer.
key_quote: Once the offeree has embarked on performance it is too late for the offeror to withdraw his offer.
full_summary: The defendant landowners made a unilateral offer: if the plaintiff came to the office the next morning with a banker's draft and a signed contract, they would exchange contracts for the sale of property. The plaintiff duly arrived with the required documents. The defendants refused to exchange, having sold to another party. The Court of Appeal held that although the defendants were entitled to revoke the unilateral offer before performance began, once the plaintiff had commenced performance — by arriving with the required documents — revocation was no longer possible. An implied term in unilateral contracts prevents revocation after the offeree has embarked on the requested performance. Daulia is the leading English authority on revocation of unilateral offers after commencement of performance, complementing Errington v Errington and important for the FE-1 on unilateral contracts.

case_name: Byrne & Co v Van Tienhoven & Co
legal_principle: Revocation of an offer is only effective when communicated to the offeree. Where an offeror posts a revocation, the revocation takes effect only when received by the offeree, not when posted. If the offeree accepts the offer before receiving the revocation, a binding contract is formed.
key_quote: The revocation of an offer has no effect until it is communicated to the person to whom the offer is made.
full_summary: On 1 October, Van Tienhoven posted an offer to Byrne in New York for tin plates. On 8 October, Van Tienhoven posted a revocation. On 11 October, Byrne received the offer and telegraphed acceptance. On 20 October, Byrne received the posted revocation. The court held a binding contract was formed on 11 October when Byrne telegraphed acceptance. Revocation is not effective on posting (unlike acceptance under the postal rule in Adams v Lindsell); it takes effect only when received. Since Byrne had not received the revocation before accepting, the offer was still open and acceptance was valid. Byrne v Van Tienhoven is the cornerstone authority on revocation: it establishes that revocation follows a 'receipt rule' rather than a 'postal rule,' forming an important contrast with Adams v Lindsell for FE-1.

legal_principle: A promise to keep an offer open (an option) is binding only if supported by consideration. Without consideration given for the option itself, the promise to keep the offer open is a bare undertaking, and the offeror may revoke at any time before acceptance.
key_quote: Without consideration for the promise to keep the offer open, the option was a bare undertaking and could be revoked at any time.
full_summary: Glass granted Walker an option to purchase land, promising to keep the offer open for a defined period. No consideration was given by Walker specifically for the option (the right to keep the offer open). Glass subsequently revoked the option before Walker sought to exercise it. Walker argued the revocation was ineffective during the option period. The Northern Ireland Court of Appeal held that an option is only binding—irrevocable—if the offeree provides consideration for the promise to keep the offer open. Without such consideration, the option is a bare gratuitous promise and may be revoked. Walker v Glass is important for Irish and Northern Irish FE-1 on the option doctrine and the need for consideration to make an offer irrevocable. It complements Dickinson v Dodds (which held an option without consideration is revocable) in the Irish/UK context.

case_name: Chwee Kin Keong v Digilandmall.com Pte Ltd
legal_principle: Where a vendor mistakenly lists goods on a website at a grossly undervalued price through human or computer error, and the purchaser knew or ought to have known of the error, the contract may be void for unilateral mistake at common law or set aside in equity. Online contract formation follows general contract principles: a website displaying goods constitutes an invitation to treat; placing an order is an offer; confirmation by the vendor is acceptance.
key_quote: If a party knows that the other is mistaken about a fundamental term … the law will not allow that party to take advantage of the error.
full_summary: Digilandmall.com mistakenly listed Hewlett-Packard laser printers at S$66 instead of S$3,854. The plaintiffs quickly ordered 1,606 units between them, suspecting the price was wrong. The website sent automated email confirmations. When the vendor refused to honour the orders, the plaintiffs sued for breach of contract. The Singapore Court of Appeal held that despite the automated confirmations, the contracts were void for unilateral mistake: the plaintiffs knew or ought to have known that the price was erroneous. The law will not allow a party to snap up an offer knowing that a fundamental mistake has been made. The case is important for FE-1 in addressing internet contract formation, automated acceptance, and the application of mistake doctrine to e-commerce. It illustrates how traditional principles apply to online commercial dealings.

legal_principle: Where one party to a contract makes a slip or error in stating the terms, and the other party knows or ought to know of the error, no contract is formed. The offeree cannot snap up an offer knowing it has been made under a mistake; enforcing such a contract would be unconscionable.
key_quote: The plaintiff must have realised … that a mistake had occurred in the offer … He could not take advantage of that obvious error.
full_summary: Colin & Shields, Argentine hare skin merchants, mistakenly offered to sell hare skins at a price stated per pound rather than per piece—the industry norm being per piece. At the per-pound rate, the skins were a fraction of their market value. Hartog, knowing the industry custom and the obvious error, accepted the offer immediately. The court held no binding contract was formed. Hartog knew the seller had made a mistake; no reasonable person in that trade would believe the offer was genuine at that price. Enforcing the agreement would have been unconscionable. Hartog v Colin & Shields is one of the most important unilateral mistake cases for FE-1: it establishes that a party who knows the other side has made a mistake cannot exploit that error. It is distinguished from cases where the mistake is merely suspected.

legal_principle: Consideration may consist of the promisee refraining from doing something they have a legal right to do (forbearance to exercise a legal right). A nephew's abstention from alcohol, tobacco, swearing, and card playing in exchange for a promise of money constitutes valid consideration, as the forbearance from lawful activities has legal value even if no tangible benefit passes to the promisor.
key_quote: A waiver of any legal right at the request of another party is a sufficient consideration for a promise.
full_summary: William Story Sr promised his nephew $5,000 if the nephew would refrain from drinking, using tobacco, swearing, and playing cards until his 21st birthday. The nephew complied. Story Sr died without paying. The executor, Sidway, refused to pay, arguing the nephew had suffered no legal detriment—the abstentions were beneficial to him. The New York Court of Appeals held the promise was supported by valid consideration: the nephew's forbearance from legal rights he possessed (the right to drink, smoke, swear, play cards) was sufficient, regardless of whether the forbearance benefited him or harmed the promisor. Hamer v Sidway is the most frequently cited American case on the sufficiency of consideration as forbearance. It is used in FE-1 to illustrate that detriment to the promisee alone suffices as consideration.

legal_principle: A promise to pay a person for doing something they are already legally obliged to do under a public duty does not constitute good consideration. The performance of a pre-existing public duty—such as attending court pursuant to a subpoena—is not fresh consideration.
key_quote: If it be a duty imposed by law upon a party to attend [court], the promise to him … is a promise without consideration.
full_summary: Godefroy promised to pay Collins, a policeman, six guineas a day to attend court and give evidence on Godefroy's behalf. Collins had already been subpoenaed and was legally obliged to attend. Godefroy later refused to pay, arguing no consideration was provided as Collins was merely doing his public duty. The court held the promise was unenforceable for want of consideration: Collins was already obliged by subpoena to attend court; his attendance was the performance of a pre-existing public duty and could not serve as fresh consideration for the promise of payment. Collins v Godefroy is the leading case on the public duty rule—the first limb of the existing duty rule. Compare with Glasbrook Brothers v Glamorgan (where exceeding the public duty does constitute consideration). Important for FE-1.

case_name: Glasbrook Brothers Ltd v Glamorgan County Council
legal_principle: Where police provide services beyond what is strictly required by their public duty—such as billeting officers on private premises rather than operating a mobile patrol—the additional service constitutes good consideration for a promise of payment. A party who exceeds their public duty in response to a request provides good consideration.
key_quote: If a person goes beyond the mere discharge of his duty, doing something that he was not legally required to do, he does something that amounts to consideration.
full_summary: During a miners' strike, Glasbrook Brothers asked the police to billet officers on their colliery for protection. The police superintendent was willing to provide mobile patrols (his public duty) but agreed to the billet arrangement only on payment. Glasbrook Brothers paid but later sought recovery. The House of Lords held the payment was binding: the police went beyond their strict public duty by providing a stationary billet rather than mobile patrols. That extra service constituted valid consideration. This case complements Collins v Godefroy (performance of existing public duty = no consideration) by establishing the exception: where a public body goes beyond its duty, the excess performance is good consideration. Important for FE-1 in analysing public duty and consideration.

legal_principle: Consideration must not be past. A promise made after a contract has been fully performed cannot be supported by the already-completed performance as consideration. Past consideration is no consideration: a representation made after sale of a horse ('the horse is sound') cannot be supported by the sale consideration because the sale was already complete.
key_quote: A consideration past and executed will support no promise. The sale was complete before the warranty was given.
full_summary: Thomas sold Roscorla a horse. After the sale was complete, Thomas promised that the horse was 'sound and free from vice.' The horse turned out to be vicious and Roscorla sued for breach of the warranty. The court held the promise was unenforceable: the sale consideration (the purchase price) was past consideration for the later warranty—the sale had been completed before the warranty was given, so it could not support the new promise. Roscorla v Thomas is the classic case establishing that past consideration is no consideration. Compare Lampleigh v Braithwait, where an act done at the promisor's request before the promise creates an implied undertaking to pay, saving the case from the past consideration rule. Key for FE-1.

legal_principle: Part payment of a debt, if made at the creditor's request on an earlier date or at a different place than stipulated, can be good satisfaction for the whole debt. However, part payment alone, on the due date and at the due place, is not sufficient satisfaction for the whole debt—the rule later known as 'accord without satisfaction' applies, making such arrangements unenforceable without fresh consideration.
key_quote: Payment of a lesser sum on the day in satisfaction of a greater cannot be any satisfaction for the whole.
full_summary: Cole owed Pinnel money due on 11 November. At Pinnel's request, Cole paid a lesser sum on 1 October. Pinnel later sued for the balance. The court held that paying the lesser sum earlier, at the creditor's request, was good satisfaction—the earlier payment was fresh consideration. However, the court stated the general principle: mere part payment of the same debt on the same day at the same place is not good consideration for forgiveness of the balance. This principle was confirmed in Foakes v Beer [1884] AC 605 (HL). Pinnel's Case is foundational for FE-1 on the part-payment rule, later partially mitigated by promissory estoppel (High Trees, D&C Builders). The case defines the orthodoxy against which Williams v Roffey and the estoppel cases are measured.

legal_principle: An arrangement between members of a household to share competition entries and to divide any prize won may be legally binding if it was a joint venture of a commercial nature even though made between domestic parties. The context is domestic but the subject matter and mutual expectations may give rise to legal enforceability.
key_quote: There was … a joint enterprise to which each contributed and from which each was to benefit.
full_summary: Mrs Pays, her granddaughter, and Simpkins (a lodger) together filled in a Sunday newspaper fashion competition each week. They agreed that any prize won would be shared equally. Mrs Pays won a prize and refused to share it with Simpkins, arguing the arrangement was a domestic agreement not intended to be legally binding. The court held the arrangement was legally binding. Although made in a domestic setting, the agreement was a joint venture involving a commercial element (the competition and prize money). Mutual promises to contribute entries and share prizes in a regular, organised way constituted a legally enforceable arrangement. Simpkins v Pays occupies an interesting position between the domestic presumption (Balfour v Balfour) and commercial presumption—it shows the presumption in domestic cases can be rebutted by sufficiently clear commercial intentions. Important for FE-1.

case_name: Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Malaysia Mining Corporation Bhd
legal_principle: A 'comfort letter' from a parent company stating 'it is our policy to ensure that [subsidiary] is at all times in a position to meet its liabilities' is a statement of present policy, not a contractual promise. It does not give rise to a legally binding obligation to ensure the subsidiary's solvency, as the court must be satisfied that the parties intended legal relations.
key_quote: A comfort letter expressing present policy is a representation, not a contractual promise, and does not give rise to legal liability.
full_summary: Malaysia Mining Corporation Bhd (MMC) sent Kleinwort Benson a comfort letter in connection with loan facilities to MMC's subsidiary, Metals Ltd, assuring the bank of the parent's policy to meet the subsidiary's liabilities. When Metals Ltd went into liquidation, Kleinwort sought to enforce the letter against MMC. The Court of Appeal held the letter was not a contractual promise: it stated present company policy (which could change), not an obligation to ensure the subsidiary's solvency. MMC lacked intention to create legal relations for this commitment. The letter was a moral, not legal, assurance. Kleinwort Benson v MMC is the leading English authority on comfort letters and is important for FE-1 in distinguishing contractual undertakings from non-binding statements of intention or policy.

legal_principle: An agreement to agree or a letter of intent may be contractually binding if it is sufficiently certain and complete in its terms and the parties have clearly intended to be bound. Work carried out under a letter of intent may found a claim in quantum meruit even if no binding contract is ultimately concluded.
key_quote: Where work has been carried out pursuant to a letter of intent, the party carrying out the work may recover on a quantum meruit basis even if no binding contract was concluded.
full_summary: AC Controls Ltd carried out preparatory works for the BBC under a letter of intent while negotiations for the full contract continued. The full contract was never executed. The BBC argued there was no binding contract and sought to pay only a fraction of the sums claimed. The court held that, although the letter of intent did not give rise to a complete binding contract for the full works, it did give rise to an obligation to pay for work actually done in reliance on the letter. The claim succeeded in quantum meruit. The case is significant for FE-1 as it illustrates the legal effect of letters of intent in construction and commercial contexts, the relationship between letters of intent and quantum meruit claims, and the analysis of incomplete contracts.

legal_principle: Where negotiations for a sale of land result in a signed 'subject to contract' document, no binding contract is formed until formal contracts are exchanged and the condition is satisfied. The 'subject to contract' formula makes clear that neither party intends to be bound until contracts are formally exchanged.
key_quote: The words 'subject to contract' negative any intention to be immediately bound; no contract arises until formal contracts are exchanged.
full_summary: Commane and Walsh negotiated the sale of land. An agreement was reached and signed, headed 'subject to contract.' Walsh subsequently withdrew. Commane sought to enforce the agreement as a binding contract. The Supreme Court of Ireland held that the 'subject to contract' qualification prevented any binding contract from arising: both parties understood that they were not to be bound until formal contracts were exchanged and signed. The phrase is a recognised signal in conveyancing practice that negotiations are continuing and that neither party accepts legal obligations until a formal contract is concluded. Commane v Walsh is the Irish authority confirming that the 'subject to contract' formula prevents premature contractual obligations and complements the English cases on the same issue. Important for Irish FE-1 on formation in land transactions.

case_name: Howberry Lane Ltd v Telecom Éireann
legal_principle: In Ireland, the contents of a telephone directory do not constitute a contractual offer by Telecom Éireann. A directory entry is at most an invitation to treat; the listing of a business number creates no binding contract to provide or maintain that service in any particular form. Liability for inaccurate directory listings must be assessed against any express terms of the service contract.
key_quote: A directory listing is an invitation to treat and does not constitute an offer that can be accepted by a caller; any liability arises from the terms of the service agreement.
full_summary: Howberry Lane, a business, suffered loss when its telephone number was incorrectly listed in the Telecom Éireann directory, causing potential customers to be unable to reach it. Howberry Lane sought damages based on the directory entry as a representation or contractual term. The Supreme Court considered the nature of directory entries and held that a directory listing is not an offer capable of acceptance; it is an invitation providing information. The liability framework depended on the terms of Telecom Éireann's service agreement with the subscriber, not on any implied contract created by the listing. The case is relevant for FE-1 in the context of distinguishing contractual offers from information and in the analysis of liability for service failures in telecommunications contracts.

case_name: Persona Digital Telephony Ltd v Minister for Public Enterprise
legal_principle: A litigation funding agreement by which a third party funds litigation in return for a share of the proceeds is champertous under Irish law and void as contrary to public policy. Unlike England and Wales (where champerty has been abolished by statute in certain contexts), the Supreme Court of Ireland affirmed the traditional rule that maintenance and champerty remain contrary to Irish public policy.
key_quote: Champerty remains unlawful in Ireland … an agreement by which a stranger to litigation finances it in return for a share of the proceeds is void.
full_summary: Persona Digital sought damages against the State for the alleged improper award of a mobile phone licence in 1996. To fund the litigation, Persona entered into a third-party funding arrangement with a litigation funder who would receive a substantial share of any recovery. The defendant challenged the funding agreement as champertous. The Supreme Court of Ireland held that maintenance (supporting another's litigation) and champerty (doing so in return for a share of the proceeds) remain contrary to Irish public policy and are therefore unlawful and void. The Court declined to follow the English legislative changes (Courts and Legal Services Act 1990) that had liberalised this area. The case is significant for Irish contract and commercial law, confirming that third-party litigation funding agreements remain unenforceable in Ireland, and is relevant for FE-1 on illegality and public policy.

legal_principle: Airline conditions of carriage that purport to exclude liability for delay or loss do not, without more, satisfy the requirements of reasonableness under the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 (Ireland) or the UCTA-equivalent Irish consumer protection framework. Exclusion clauses in consumer contracts are subject to a reasonableness test and must be brought to the consumer's attention.
key_quote: A standard exclusion clause in an airline contract of carriage will be assessed for reasonableness; the burden of proving reasonableness lies on the party relying on the clause.
full_summary: O'Keefe brought proceedings against Ryanair following delays and loss of luggage. Ryanair sought to rely on exclusion clauses in its conditions of carriage. The Irish High Court analysed the validity of the exclusion clauses under the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 and general Irish contract law. The Court held that exclusion clauses in consumer transport contracts are subject to a requirement of reasonableness and must be adequately brought to the attention of the consumer. Where the clause purports to exclude all liability for consequential losses arising from delay, this may fail the reasonableness test in a consumer context. The case illustrates the application of Irish consumer protection legislation to standard-form airline contracts and is relevant for FE-1 on exclusion clauses and statutory reasonableness in Ireland.

case_name: Grehan v North East Health Board
legal_principle: A public body in Ireland may be bound by its undertakings and representations where those undertakings satisfy the conditions of a binding contract, including offer, acceptance, and consideration. The fact that a party is a public body does not prevent contractual obligations arising; the context of employment may generate legitimate expectations and binding commitments.
key_quote: The Health Board's undertaking … was capable of creating a contractually binding obligation enforceable against it.
full_summary: Grehan was a nurse employed by the North East Health Board. She had taken a career break on the basis of assurances from the Board that her position would be held open and she could return. When she sought to return, the Board refused to reinstate her in the agreed manner. The Supreme Court of Ireland held that the Board's assurances, made in the context of an employment relationship, could give rise to binding contractual obligations. The case is significant for Irish public employment law and for FE-1 in analysing whether representations by public employers create contractually enforceable rights, and the interaction of promissory estoppel and contractual obligation in the public sector context.

legal_principle: A local authority letting a flat to a tenant under the Housing Act 1966 impliedly warrants that the flat is fit for human habitation at the time of letting. This implied warranty of fitness is a term of the tenancy contract that can be enforced against the local authority. Irish law recognises an implied term of fitness for habitation in residential lettings by local authorities.
key_quote: There is an implied term in the contract of tenancy that the premises are fit for human habitation at the commencement of the tenancy.
full_summary: Siney was allocated a flat by Dublin Corporation under its social housing function. The flat was defective and unfit for habitation due to dampness and structural problems, causing personal injury to Mrs Siney. She sued the Corporation in contract. The Supreme Court held that the letting by the Corporation impliedly warranted that the flat was fit for human habitation at the commencement of the tenancy. This implied term arose both from the Housing Act 1966 and the nature of the landlord-tenant relationship, particularly where a public authority lets to vulnerable tenants. Siney v Dublin Corporation is a leading Irish case on implied terms and on the duties of local authority landlords. It is relevant for FE-1 on implied terms in contracts and the relationship between statute and contract.

legal_principle: Irish courts will set aside a transaction between parties in a close relationship (such as siblings) where the transaction was improvident and where one party took advantage of the other's trust and confidence. The doctrine of undue influence in Irish law applies both to the presumed class (relationships of trust) and to actual undue influence. An improvident transaction between family members raises a presumption of undue influence that the dominant party must rebut.
key_quote: The circumstances here are such as to raise a presumption of undue influence which has not been rebutted; the transaction must be set aside.
full_summary: Siblings agreed to a transaction involving land under which one was significantly disadvantaged. The improvidence of the transaction and the family relationship raised the question of undue influence. The Supreme Court of Ireland held that Irish law recognises the presumption of undue influence in relationships of trust and confidence, including siblings where one occupies a position of dominance. Once a presumed relationship is established and the transaction is shown to be improvident, the burden shifts to the party in the dominant position to show the other received independent advice and acted freely. The case confirms that Irish undue influence doctrine follows the English classification (actual and presumed) and applies vigorously to family transactions. Important for FE-1 on undue influence in an Irish context.

legal_principle: An exclusion clause displayed in a hotel bedroom cannot form part of a contract that was concluded at the reception desk, as the clause was not brought to the guest's attention before the contract was made. A clause must be incorporated at or before the time of contracting to be effective; terms introduced after formation are ineffective.
key_quote: The contract had been made before the guests had noticed the notice. The exemption clause was not part of the contract.
full_summary: Mr and Mrs Olley checked into the Marlborough Court Hotel and paid in advance at the reception. When they went to their room, they saw a notice on the wall stating that the hotel was not responsible for articles lost or stolen unless handed to the manager for safekeeping. A thief who had obtained the room key stole Mrs Olley's furs. The Court of Appeal held the hotel could not rely on the exclusion notice: the contract was formed at the reception desk before the Olleys saw the bedroom notice. Terms not brought to the attention of a party before contracting are not incorporated. Olley v Marlborough Court is a foundational case on incorporation of exemption clauses for FE-1, establishing the timing rule: exclusion clauses must be incorporated at or before the moment of contracting.

legal_principle: A course of dealing can incorporate an exclusion clause into a contract only if the dealings were sufficiently consistent and numerous to give rise to a reasonable inference that the parties intended the term to form part of their contract. Three or four transactions over five years may be insufficient to establish the course of dealing necessary to incorporate a term.
key_quote: Three or four transactions over five years is not a sufficient course of dealing to incorporate an exclusion clause into a subsequent contract.
full_summary: Hollier's car was damaged by fire while being repaired at Rambler Motors. The garage had on previous occasions (three or four times over five years) sent Hollier invoices containing a clause excluding liability for fire damage. This clause was not specifically drawn to his attention at the time of the relevant contract. The Court of Appeal held the clause was not incorporated by course of dealing: the prior transactions were too infrequent and informal to give rise to a consistent course of dealing that would entitle the court to imply the clause into the subsequent contract. The case contrasts with McCutcheon v MacBrayne (where no consistent course of dealing existed) and illustrates the threshold for course of dealing incorporation. Important for FE-1 on the three methods of incorporation (signature, notice, course of dealing).

legal_principle: An exclusion clause can be incorporated into a contract by a previous course of dealing, even if the clause is on a document received after the contract was made, provided the parties regularly dealt on the same terms. The more unusual or onerous the clause, the greater the notice required to incorporate it.
key_quote: The more unreasonable a clause is, the greater the notice which must be given of it before it can be said to be incorporated.
full_summary: Bradshaw stored some barrels with Spurling, a warehouse company. A document ('landing account') was sent after delivery, containing a clause excluding liability for negligence. Bradshaw had dealt with Spurling on the same terms over many years. When the barrels were returned damaged (empty), Spurling relied on the exclusion clause. The Court of Appeal held the clause was incorporated by the long course of dealing. Lord Denning also articulated the 'red hand' principle: the more unusual or onerous a clause, the more prominently it must be flagged before a court will find it incorporated. This statement was later developed into the principle in Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes. Important for FE-1 on course of dealing and the notice required for unusual clauses.

case_name: Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes Ltd
legal_principle: Where a condition in a standard form contract is particularly onerous or unusual, the party relying on it must show that it was fairly and reasonably brought to the other party's attention before it will be incorporated into the contract. The more unusual the term, the greater the steps required to bring it to the other party's attention.
key_quote: If one condition in a set of printed conditions is particularly onerous or unusual, the party seeking to enforce it must show that that particular condition was fairly brought to the attention of the other party.
full_summary: Interfoto supplied 47 photographic transparencies on loan to Stiletto's advertising agency. The delivery note contained a condition (in small print among several others) requiring a holding charge of £5 per day per transparency for any kept beyond 14 days. Stiletto returned the transparencies 4 weeks late and Interfoto claimed £3,783. The Court of Appeal held the charge was not incorporated: the holding fee was an onerous and unusual term that was not specifically drawn to Stiletto's attention. It was not sufficient to bury it in small print. Interfoto Picture Library is the definitive authority on the 'red hand rule' for onerous clauses: terms that are unusually burdensome require greater steps to be validly incorporated. Fundamental for FE-1 on incorporation by notice.

legal_principle: An exclusion clause limiting a laundry's liability for loss or damage to goods must, on its proper construction, be read as covering negligence where negligence is the only realistic basis on which liability could arise. The main purpose rule aids construction: a limitation clause is construed to cover the only form of liability the clause could meaningfully address.
key_quote: If the words used are wide enough to cover negligence, and the only liability which the party could have is for negligence, then the clause covers negligence.
full_summary: Alderslade sent handkerchiefs to Hendon Laundry. They were lost. A clause in the contract limited the laundry's liability 'for articles lost or damaged' to 20 times the laundering charge. The laundry was negligent. Alderslade argued the clause could not cover negligence because that word was not used. The Court of Appeal held the clause did cover negligence: a laundry's only basis of liability for lost goods is negligence. To give the clause any practical effect, it had to be construed as covering the only head of liability that existed. Alderslade v Hendon Laundry is the origin of the rule later systematised in Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King [1952] AC 192 (the three-stage Canada Steamship test). Important for FE-1 on construction of exemption clauses and negligence.

case_name: Transformers and Rectifiers Ltd v Needs Ltd
legal_principle: In a 'battle of the forms' where both parties exchange standard terms, the last shot rule determines that the terms of the last set of standard conditions sent before acceptance of performance prevails, unless the course of dealings shows the parties agreed otherwise. Where neither party's standard terms are clearly last, the court may find a contract on neither party's terms.
key_quote: Where neither party's standard terms are clearly incorporated, the court must determine from the conduct of the parties what, if any, contract was concluded and on whose terms.
full_summary: Transformers and Rectifiers (TR) ordered components from Needs Ltd on TR's purchase order form, which incorporated TR's standard terms. Needs responded with its own acknowledgement form incorporating its standard terms (which excluded any liability for consequential loss). Both sets of forms were exchanged without resolution of whose terms prevailed. When the components were defective, TR claimed consequential losses. The TCC had to determine which standard terms governed the contract. The court applied the 'last shot' principle (the final set of terms sent before acceptance prevails) but found the position unclear from the parties' conduct and applied the usual rules of offer and acceptance to each communication. The case is instructive for FE-1 on the battle of the forms in commercial contracts and the uncertain outcome when neither party's standard terms are clearly adopted.

case_name: Moore & Co Ltd v Landauer & Co
legal_principle: Under a contract for the sale of goods by description, the buyer is entitled to goods that exactly correspond to the description specified, including the manner of packing where packing is part of the description. A seller who delivers the correct quantity but in different-sized cases than described commits a breach of condition, entitling the buyer to reject all goods even if their commercial value is unaffected.
key_quote: The description 'Australian canned fruits in cases of 30 tins' forms part of the contractual description … delivery in cases of 24 tins is a breach of condition.
full_summary: A contract specified the supply of Australian canned fruits in cases of 30 tins each. Landauer delivered the correct total number of tins, but approximately half were packed in cases of 24 tins. The Court of Appeal held there was a breach of the condition as to description under the Sale of Goods Act: the specification of packing in cases of 30 formed part of the contractual description, and exact compliance was required. The fact that the actual commercial value of the goods was unaffected was irrelevant. Moore v Landauer is frequently cited in FE-1 on the strict rule of compliance with description in sale of goods contracts. It is sometimes contrasted with Arcos v Ronaasen and later decisions moving toward a de minimis approach, and with The Hansa Nord, where termination was not permitted for a minor breach classified as a warranty rather than a condition.

case_name: The Hansa Nord (Cehave NV v Bremer Handelsgesellschaft mbH)
legal_principle: Not every term in a sale of goods contract is either a condition or a warranty; some terms are innominate (intermediate) terms, and the remedy for breach depends on the seriousness of the consequences. Even where goods slightly fail to meet contractual quality requirements, the buyer cannot reject them if the breach does not go to the root of the contract — a lesser breach gives only damages.
key_quote: Where a term is neither a condition nor a warranty, the court must look at the events that have occurred as a result of the breach and ask whether they are such as to go to the root of the contract.
full_summary: Cehave purchased a cargo of citrus pulp pellets for animal feed. The contract stipulated 'shipped in good condition.' Some of the cargo was damaged. The buyers rejected the entire cargo, claiming breach of condition. The damaged cargo was later bought at auction by the buyers at a fraction of the price and used for its originally intended purpose. The Court of Appeal held the buyers could not reject: the term 'shipped in good condition' was an innominate term, not a condition. The breach's consequences had to be assessed — since the buyers were still able to use the goods for their intended purpose (though at reduced quality), the breach did not go to the root of the contract. The buyers were limited to damages. The Hansa Nord developed the innominate term category beyond Hong Kong Fir into the sale of goods context. Significant for FE-1 on classification of terms.

legal_principle: An 'expected ready to load' clause in a charterparty is a condition, not a warranty or innominate term. Breach of such a clause by the charterer entitles the shipowner to treat the contract as repudiated and claim damages, even where the breach causes no appreciable loss to the innocent party. The classification of a term as a condition depends on the nature and purpose of the term in commercial contracts.
key_quote: An 'expected ready to load' clause is a condition … breach of it entitles the innocent party to treat the contract as at an end.
full_summary: A charterparty stated that the Mihalis Angelos was 'expected ready to load about 1 July.' The actual readiness to load was delayed well beyond this date. The charterers had accepted a cargo cancellation message from their own clients and purported to cancel the charterparty. The Court of Appeal held the 'expected ready to load' clause was a condition of the charterparty: its breach entitled the charterers to treat the contract as repudiated and claim damages (even nominal, since they had an independent right to cancel). The Mihalis Angelos is important for FE-1 in demonstrating how courts classify terms in commercial contracts — particularly charterparties — and confirming that anticipatory breach and termination for breach of condition are distinct doctrines that can overlap.

case_name: Harlingdon & Leinster Enterprises Ltd v Christopher Hull Fine Art Ltd
legal_principle: For a sale to be a 'sale by description' under the Sale of Goods Act 1979, the description must be a term of the contract that the buyer relies upon. Where the buyer is at least as expert as the seller, buys the goods on their own inspection, and does not rely on a description stated by the seller, there is no sale by description and no implied condition that the goods correspond to the description.
key_quote: There is no sale by description … if the buyer does not rely on the description as a contractual term … the seller's attribution did not influence the decision to buy.
full_summary: Christopher Hull Fine Art sold a painting described (erroneously) as by Gabriele Münter. Harlingdon's agent, also an expert in German expressionist art, inspected the painting and purchased it without relying on the attribution as a guarantee of authenticity. The painting later proved not to be by Münter. The Court of Appeal held there was no sale by description: description as to artist became a term of the contract only if the buyer relied on it. Since Harlingdon's specialist made their own assessment and did not contract on the basis of Hull's attribution, the implied condition under s.13 SGA did not apply. The case is important for FE-1 in defining when the description implied term in s.13 Sale of Goods Act applies, particularly in specialist contexts where reliance on description may be absent.

case_name: Wallis Son & Wells v Pratt & Haynes
legal_principle: An exclusion clause that excludes liability for breach of warranty does not exclude liability for breach of condition. A seller cannot rely on a clause excluding warranties to avoid liability for breach of the condition as to correspondence with description under the Sale of Goods Act. Exclusion clauses are construed strictly and do not extend beyond their express terms.
key_quote: The clause excluding liability for warranties did not exclude the breach of condition as to description; the two are fundamentally different.
full_summary: Pratt sold seeds described as 'common English sainfoin' to Wallis. The seeds supplied were in fact giant sainfoin—a different variety. The contract excluded 'warranties,' but not conditions. Under the Sale of Goods Act, the requirement that goods correspond with their description is a condition. When Wallis sued, Pratt relied on the warranty exclusion clause. The House of Lords held the clause did not protect the seller: the implied obligation as to correspondence with description is a condition, not a warranty. A clause excluding warranties cannot be extended to exclude conditions; the words used must be given their strict contractual meaning. Wallis v Pratt is important for FE-1 on the distinction between conditions and warranties and on the strict construction of exemption clauses.

legal_principle: A representation that is literally true but incomplete, creating a misleading impression, is a misrepresentation — sometimes called a 'half-truth.' Partial disclosure of facts that makes a statement literally accurate but misleading overall can found an action for misrepresentation.
key_quote: A statement which is literally true but which is misleading because it suppresses material facts amounts to a misrepresentation.
full_summary: Hallett, selling land, stated that the farms on the estate were 'fully let.' This was literally true — the farms were let — but Hallett failed to disclose that the tenants were on the point of leaving, having given notice to quit. Dimmock, the purchaser, sought to set aside the contract on the ground of misrepresentation. The Court of Appeal held the statement was a misrepresentation: while true as far as it went, the suppression of the material fact that the tenants were leaving made it a half-truth. A representor cannot say 'I merely stated the facts' while suppressing information that gives those facts a radically different character. Dimmock v Hallett establishes the doctrine of 'half-truths' in misrepresentation law and is important for FE-1 alongside Smith v Land & House Property Corp and With v O'Flanagan.

legal_principle: A statement of intention to act in a particular way is not a misrepresentation if the intention was honestly held at the time of the statement. A subsequent change of intention does not render the original statement false; contrast this with cases where the intention was false at the time it was stated.
key_quote: The wife's statement of her intention not to remarry was honestly made; her subsequent change of mind did not amount to a misrepresentation.
full_summary: On separation, Mrs Wadham told her husband she did not intend to remarry. This statement influenced the maintenance agreement the parties concluded. She subsequently remarried. Mr Wadham sought to set aside the agreement as founded on misrepresentation. The court held there was no actionable misrepresentation: Mrs Wadham had honestly held the stated intention at the time she made it. Her later change of mind did not make the original statement false. The test for misrepresentation of intention (following Edgington v Fitzmaurice) is whether the intention was honestly held at the time. Compare With v O'Flanagan: where a representation remains true when made but circumstances change before contracting, there may be a duty to correct. Wales v Wadham is relevant for FE-1 in distinguishing honest changes of intention from false statements of intention.

legal_principle: For an action in deceit (fraudulent misrepresentation), the claimant must prove that the misrepresentation was material and was actually relied upon. If the claimant would have acted in the same way regardless of the representation, there is no causation and the action fails. Reliance must be proved; a claimant who did not actually act on the representation cannot recover.
key_quote: The plaintiff must prove that he actually relied on the misrepresentation … if he would have acted the same way without it, his action fails.
full_summary: The prospectus of a company stated that a well-known person was a member of the Parliamentary Committee of the company. This was false. The plaintiff subscribed for shares. However, evidence showed the plaintiff had not actually read the prospectus in detail and would have subscribed regardless. The House of Lords held the action failed: even if a misrepresentation is fraudulent, the plaintiff must prove actual reliance — that the misrepresentation induced the transaction. If the claimant would have acted the same way regardless of the misrepresentation, the causal link is broken. Smith v Chadwick is fundamental to the FE-1 misrepresentation analysis: it is the leading authority on the reliance (inducement) requirement. Without proof of actual reliance, even a fraudulent misrepresentation will not give rise to liability.

case_name: Pan Atlantic Insurance Co Ltd v Pine Top Insurance Co Ltd
legal_principle: In contracts of insurance (uberrimae fidei), the insured's duty of disclosure requires disclosure of every material fact—defined as a fact that would influence the judgment of a prudent insurer—but avoidance for non-disclosure also requires that the non-disclosure actually induced the contract. Mere materiality is insufficient; the insurer must show they were actually induced by the non-disclosure.
key_quote: Materiality and inducement are distinct requirements … the insurer must show not merely that a fact was material, but that the non-disclosure induced the making of the contract.
full_summary: Pine Top (reinsurer) sought to avoid a reinsurance policy on grounds that Pan Atlantic had failed to disclose a poor claims record. The House of Lords held that while the duty of disclosure in insurance contracts (utmost good faith) requires disclosure of all material facts, avoidance also requires proof of actual inducement: the insurer must show that they were actually influenced by the non-disclosed matter. Materiality alone (applying the 'prudent insurer' test) is necessary but not sufficient — the actual insurer must have been induced. This two-stage test (materiality + inducement) governs non-disclosure in insurance contracts under English law and is relevant for FE-1 when considering uberrimae fidei contracts and misrepresentation/non-disclosure in the insurance context.

legal_principle: Even where a common mistake does not render a contract void at common law (because the mistake does not make performance fundamentally different from what was contracted), a court of equity has jurisdiction to set aside the contract on terms if the mistake was fundamental and it would be unconscionable to hold the parties to the agreement. The equitable jurisdiction provides a broader remedy for mistake than the common law.
key_quote: Even if the contract is good at common law, it may yet be set aside in equity … where the common mistake was fundamental and it would be inequitable to hold the parties to their agreement.
full_summary: Solle and Butcher both believed that a flat was not a controlled tenancy under the Rent Acts and agreed a rent well above the controlled level. In fact the flat was controlled, so the rent was illegal. Butcher sought rescission, arguing common mistake. The Court of Appeal held the contract could not be void at common law (Bell v Lever Brothers standard not met), but equity could set it aside: both parties had contracted under a fundamental common mistake about the legal status of the flat and it would be unconscionable to hold them to the contract. The contract was set aside on terms that Solle could take a new lease at a lawful rent. Solle v Butcher was subsequently questioned by Great Peace Shipping [2003] QB 679 (which denied an independent equitable jurisdiction for common mistake), creating uncertainty that persists. Important for FE-1 in comparing common law and equitable approaches to mistake.

legal_principle: A separation deed entered into between a man and woman who both believed themselves to be married, but who in fact were not married (the marriage being void), is void for common mistake as to a fundamental fact — the existence of the marriage. The agreement is founded on a non-existent state of affairs.
key_quote: The agreement to separate was founded upon the common assumption that the parties were married … that assumption was false, and the agreement was therefore void.
full_summary: A man and woman believed they were married and entered into a separation deed regulating their rights on separation. It subsequently emerged that the man's first wife was still alive at the time of the 'marriage,' making the second marriage void. The woman sought to enforce the separation deed. The King's Bench held the deed was void for common mistake: both parties had contracted on the fundamental assumption that they were married, and that assumption was false. The very subject matter of the agreement (a valid marriage to separate from) did not exist. Galloway v Galloway is an example of res extincta/res sua mistake applied to personal status and is relevant for FE-1 in illustrating common mistake as to the existence of the factual matrix of the contract.

legal_principle: Where parties contract for the sale of specific goods that, unknown to both parties, have already perished at the time of contracting, the contract is void (res extincta). There is no agreement to sell goods that do not exist, and the seller cannot be held to a promise to pass title in goods that had already ceased to exist.
key_quote: The contract … was for the sale of an existing something … the purchaser was not bound by a contract for the purchase of goods which had already ceased to exist.
full_summary: A cargo of corn was shipped from Salonica to England. A commercial agent sold the cargo documents to Hastie while the cargo was at sea. Unknown to both parties, the cargo had already been disposed of by the ship's captain (it had fermented and been sold at a port en route). Hastie refused to pay. The House of Lords held Hastie was not liable: the contract was for the sale of an existing cargo. Since the cargo had already perished before the contract was made, there were no goods to which the contract could attach. The contract was void. Couturier v Hastie is the foundation of the res extincta doctrine in English contract law — if goods do not exist at the time of contracting, no contract can be made. This principle is now codified in section 6 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979. Fundamental for FE-1 on common mistake.

legal_principle: Where parties negotiate a contract face to face, there is a presumption that the offeror intended to deal with the person physically present, not with some other person whose identity the other party fraudulently assumed. This presumption may be rebutted where the identity of the contracting party was of vital importance such that, but for the fraudulent misrepresentation, no contract would have been made.
key_quote: The presumption that a party intends to deal with the person in front of them may be rebutted if identity is of paramount importance to the formation of the contract.
full_summary: The Misses Ingram advertised their car for sale. A rogue called claiming to be 'Mr Hutchinson of Chadwick Road, Guildford.' The Ingrams checked the phone book, found the name listed, and agreed to accept a cheque from 'Mr Hutchinson.' The cheque bounced. The rogue had sold the car to Little. The Ingrams sued Little in conversion, arguing the contract with the rogue was void for mistake as to identity. The Court of Appeal held (by majority) the contract was void: the Ingrams had attached vital importance to the identity of the buyer (they would not have accepted a cheque from a stranger), and the rogue's assumption of Hutchinson's identity prevented any contract forming. This is a controversial decision that sits in tension with Lewis v Averay. Together Ingram and Lewis illustrate the distinction between identity (void for mistake) and creditworthiness (merely voidable for misrepresentation). Critical for FE-1 on mistake as to identity.

legal_principle: Irish courts will assess the validity of mortgage contracts and related documentation applying Irish contract law principles, including formation, misrepresentation, and undue influence. Mortgagors may challenge mortgage deeds where independent legal advice was not properly given and where the circumstances suggest that consent was not freely and fully informed.
key_quote: Where independent legal advice was inadequate and the mortgagor's consent was not freely and fully informed, a mortgage may be set aside on grounds of undue influence or unconscionable dealing.
full_summary: The Hayes challenged AIB's enforcement of a mortgage over their family home, arguing that they had not received adequate independent legal advice before executing the mortgage documentation. The Irish Court of Appeal examined whether the requirements for informed consent had been met and whether the bank had constructive notice of circumstances that should have led it to ensure proper independent advice was taken. The court applied principles from the English line (Barclays Bank v O'Brien; Etridge) adapted to Irish conditions. The case underscores the importance of proper independent legal advice in mortgage transactions and the consequences for lenders of constructive notice of potential undue influence. Important for FE-1 on third-party undue influence in an Irish banking context.

legal_principle: A bank is put on constructive notice of potential undue influence or misrepresentation when a wife (or cohabitant) stands as surety for her husband's debts without direct financial benefit to herself. To escape the consequences of that notice, the bank must take reasonable steps to ensure the surety has received adequate independent legal advice and understands the transaction.
key_quote: If a creditor … knows that a surety is a wife standing surety for her husband's debts, it has constructive notice of the possible undue influence … and must take steps to bring home the risk to the surety.
full_summary: Mrs O'Brien charged the matrimonial home as security for her husband's business overdraft. She claimed she signed the mortgage documents without understanding their nature, following her husband's misrepresentation of the extent of the liability. The bank had not ensured she received independent advice. The House of Lords held that where a bank knows it is taking a surety from a spouse who receives no direct benefit, it has constructive notice of the risk of misrepresentation or undue influence. To be free from equity's intervention, the bank must take reasonable steps—typically ensuring independent legal advice is received. Barclays v O'Brien is the foundational case in English and Irish law on third-party undue influence in surety transactions, extended and systematised in Etridge. Fundamental for FE-1.

legal_principle: A gift or transaction made by a person under a presumed relationship of undue influence — such as a devotee to a religious superior — will be set aside unless the donor freely acted with independent advice. However, the equitable right to rescission may be lost by delay (laches) if the claimant delays unreasonably in bringing the claim after the relationship of influence has ended.
key_quote: A gift by a person who is under the influence of another to whom she stands in a relation of entire confidence … will be presumed to have been obtained by undue influence and will be set aside unless she had independent advice.
full_summary: Miss Allcard became a member of a Protestant sisterhood governed by Sister Eva Skinner. The sisterhood rules required members to give all property to the sisterhood. Allcard transferred virtually her entire assets under this rule. Some years after leaving the sisterhood, she sought to recover. The Court of Appeal held the gifts had been made under a presumed relationship of undue influence (spiritual superior and devotee), creating a presumption that must be rebutted. However, the claim was barred by laches: Allcard had known about her right to claim for years after leaving the sisterhood and had delayed bringing proceedings. Allcard v Skinner is fundamental for FE-1 in establishing both Class 2A presumed undue influence and the defence of delay/laches to equitable remedies.

legal_principle: A contract may be set aside where it is entered into under circumstances of 'inequality of bargaining power' and where one party used that inequality unconscionably. Lord Denning proposed a general principle of inequality of bargaining power as the unifying rationale behind undue influence, duress, and unconscionability, though this general principle has not been adopted by subsequent courts.
key_quote: When one party has such superior bargaining power that the weaker party has no real choice but to agree … the court may set aside the transaction as unconscionable.
full_summary: Bundy, an elderly farmer, mortgaged his home to guarantee his son's business debts with Lloyds Bank. The bank manager attended the farm without any independent adviser present for the father, and the father signed the guarantee under pressure. Lord Denning in the Court of Appeal articulated a broad principle that courts can set aside contracts where there is inequality of bargaining power used unconscionably, unifying undue influence, economic duress, and unconscionable dealing under one general heading. On the particular facts, the bank's representative acting for both the bank (creditor) and the father (surety) without ensuring independent advice constituted actual undue influence. The case was decided on undue influence, and Lord Denning's general principle was doubted in National Westminster Bank v Morgan [1985]. Important for FE-1 as a vehicle for discussing the boundaries of undue influence and unconscionable bargain.

case_name: Schroeder Music Publishing Co Ltd v Macaulay
legal_principle: A standard form contract in restraint of trade that is wholly one-sided and drafted by the party with superior bargaining power—particularly in the music industry—may be struck down as unreasonable restraint of trade. The court will examine whether the restraint is reasonable as between the parties (protecting a legitimate interest proportionately) and in the public interest.
key_quote: The test of reasonableness … requires the court to consider whether the restrictions are more than adequate to protect the legitimate interest of the promisee.
full_summary: Macaulay, a young unknown songwriter, signed a five-year exclusive agreement with Schroeder Music. The agreement gave Schroeder extensive rights over all his works, allowed termination by the company at any time but not by Macaulay, and gave Schroeder no obligation to exploit the songs. Macaulay sought a declaration that the agreement was void for restraint of trade. The House of Lords unanimously held the agreement was an unreasonable restraint of trade: it was grossly one-sided, imposed on an inexperienced party by a powerful company using its standard form. It did not protect any legitimate business interest of Schroeder proportionately. Schroeder Music v Macaulay is the leading case on restraint of trade in entertainment contracts and is important for FE-1 on the doctrine of restraint of trade beyond employment contexts.

legal_principle: A solus agreement (exclusive dealing tie) between an oil company and a garage owner is subject to the doctrine of restraint of trade. Such agreements are reasonable (and enforceable) only if they protect a legitimate interest of the oil supplier and the restriction imposed is reasonable in duration, geographical scope, and degree. An agreement that unduly restricts the garage owner's freedom to deal is void to the extent it is unreasonable.
key_quote: A solus agreement is a contract in restraint of trade and is enforceable only if it is reasonable in the interests of the parties and the public.
full_summary: Martin, a garage proprietor, entered into a solus agreement with Petrofina requiring him to purchase only Petrofina's petroleum products for 12 years. He later wished to buy from another supplier. The Court of Appeal confirmed that solus (exclusive dealing) agreements are subject to the restraint of trade doctrine. To be enforceable they must be reasonable: the court balances the oil company's legitimate interest in securing retail outlets against the restriction imposed on the garage owner's commercial freedom. A 12-year solus tie was considered excessive and unreasonable. The case is important for FE-1 on restraint of trade in commercial (non-employment) contexts and illustrates that the doctrine applies to a variety of commercial arrangements beyond employment covenants.

legal_principle: A court may sever an unreasonable part of a restraint of trade clause if the offending words can be removed without altering the fundamental nature of the remaining obligation. The 'blue pencil test' (applying the three conditions of Beckett v CAV Ltd) permits severance where removal of the offending words leaves the remainder of the clause both grammatically coherent and reasonable. Importantly, the Supreme Court overruled the rule that severance is impermissible if it would change the character of the post-employment obligation.
key_quote: Severance of unreasonable words is permissible if … it does not generate any major change in the overall effect of the post-employment restraints in the contract.
full_summary: Tillman's employment contract with Egon Zehnder contained a 6-month post-employment non-compete clause preventing her from being 'interested in' a competitor. She argued the phrase 'interested in' was too wide (it would prohibit even minority shareholdings) and rendered the entire clause void. The Supreme Court held the words 'interested in' could be severed (blue-pencilled), leaving a valid non-solicitation and non-dealing clause. The Court modernised the severance test: severance is permissible if the remaining clause reads sensibly, changes character only minimally, and removal does not require the court to rewrite the clause. Tillman v Egon Zehnder is the leading modern authority on severance in restraint of trade clauses and is significant for FE-1 in employment and commercial law contexts.

legal_principle: Under an entire obligations contract, a party is entitled to payment only on complete performance; partial performance does not give rise to any right to payment. Where a contract expressly requires complete performance as a condition of payment, the death or incapacity of a party performing before completion gives rise to no right to a quantum meruit.
key_quote: The whole [sum] was payable upon the contingency of completing the whole voyage … the condition precedent to payment was entire performance.
full_summary: Cutter was appointed mate of a ship at a fixed sum to be paid 'provided he proceeded, continued, and did his duty from hence to the port of Jamaica.' Cutter died at sea before the ship reached Jamaica. His widow sued for a proportionate share of the agreed sum. The King's Bench held no payment was due: the contract was an entire obligations contract—complete performance was a condition precedent to payment. Since Cutter had not completed the voyage, no part of the agreed sum was payable, regardless of how much work he had done. Cutter v Powell is foundational for FE-1 on the doctrine of entire obligations and the rule that partial performance is no performance in entire contracts. It is contrasted with cases allowing quantum meruit for partial performance where there is acceptance of benefit (Sumpter v Hodges).

legal_principle: Where a party abandons an entire obligations contract partway through, they cannot recover on a quantum meruit for the work done unless the other party freely accepts the partial performance. Acceptance of the partial benefit is not 'free' where the defendant has no choice but to accept it (e.g., a half-built structure on their land).
key_quote: Where a man has abandoned performance of a contract, he cannot recover on a quantum meruit for work done, unless the other party has freely accepted partial performance.
full_summary: Sumpter contracted to build two houses and stables on Hodges's land for a lump sum. Having done roughly two-thirds of the work, Sumpter ran out of money and abandoned the contract. Hodges completed the buildings using materials left on site. Sumpter sued for the value of work done on a quantum meruit. The Court of Appeal held Sumpter could not recover on quantum meruit: he had abandoned an entire obligations contract, and Hodges's retention and use of the partially built structure was not 'free acceptance'—he had no real choice but to retain it since it was already built on his land. Compare Bolton v Mahadeva (where defective work is paid for less the cost of making good) and Cutter v Powell. Important for FE-1 on the limits of quantum meruit as a remedy for partial performance.

legal_principle: A contract to install central heating for a fixed lump sum is an entire obligations contract. Where the work is defective to a substantial degree, the contractor cannot recover the contract price. However, the doctrine of substantial performance may allow recovery of the price less the cost of remedying the defects, provided the shortcomings are not so serious as to defeat the main purpose of the contract.
key_quote: Where defects are so serious that performance cannot be said to be substantially complete, the contractor cannot recover the contract price.
full_summary: Bolton agreed to install a central heating system in Mahadeva's house for £560. The system was defective: it gave off fumes and provided inadequate heat. The cost of repair was estimated at £174 (about 31% of the contract price). The Court of Appeal held Bolton could not recover the contract price: the defects were so pervasive that the work could not be said to substantially comply with the contract. Unlike Hoenig v Isaacs (where minor defects allowed recovery under substantial performance), here the defects were fundamental—the central purpose of the contract (providing adequate heating and warmth) was not achieved. Bolton v Mahadeva demonstrates the limit of the substantial performance doctrine: where the deviation from the contract is too significant, the entire obligations rule applies and no payment is due.

legal_principle: Where a party to a contract announces before the time for performance that they do not intend to perform (anticipatory breach), the innocent party may treat the contract as immediately repudiated and sue for damages at once, without waiting for the date on which performance was due. The innocent party need not remain ready and willing to perform during the interim period.
key_quote: When a party gives notice of his intention not to perform before the day arrives, the other party may commence an action for damages immediately.
full_summary: De La Tour engaged Hochster as a courier for a European tour commencing 1 June. On 11 May, De La Tour wrote to Hochster saying he would not need him and discharged him from his obligations. Before 1 June, Hochster commenced an action for breach of contract. The court held the action was maintainable: once a party announces in advance that they will not perform, they commit an anticipatory breach. The innocent party may elect to accept the repudiation and sue immediately for damages. Hochster v De La Tour is the foundational case on anticipatory breach and the doctrine that allows immediate action without waiting for the contractual performance date. Contrasted with White & Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor [1962] (where the innocent party may sometimes continue performance rather than accept repudiation). Important for FE-1.
