legal_principle: A claimant may elect to claim damages on a reliance loss basis (wasted expenditure) rather than on an expectation loss basis (loss of bargain), but the reliance basis cannot be used to recover more than the claimant would have received if the contract had been performed. If the contract was a bad bargain such that the claimant would not have recovered their expenditure even if it had been performed, reliance damages are limited accordingly.
key_quote: The plaintiff cannot use the wasted expenditure basis of damages to escape from a bad bargain he has made.
full_summary: Middleton was granted a renewable licence to use premises for a business on a six-weekly basis. He spent money on improvements. When his licence was not renewed and he was ejected, he sought to recover the cost of improvements on a reliance loss basis. The Court of Appeal held he could not recover those improvements: the improvements were made to premises he had no security of tenure over and would have been lost at the end of any tenancy. Since the contract itself had only a short tenancy, the expenditure on improvements would not have been recovered anyway. The claimant cannot use reliance loss damages to put himself in a better position than he would have been in had the contract been performed. CP Haulage v Middleton is the authority for the bad bargain limitation on reliance loss claims. Important for FE-1 on contractual damages.

legal_principle: An injunction may be granted to enforce a negative covenant in a personal service contract, even though specific performance of the positive obligation to perform would not be granted. A court will restrain a party from breaking a negative promise (e.g., not to perform elsewhere) without compelling positive performance, provided the injunction would not effectively amount to specific performance or force performance against the party's will.
key_quote: I do not think I am bound to decree specific performance … but I may grant an injunction restraining her from singing for another person contrary to her agreement.
full_summary: Johanna Wagner was engaged exclusively to perform at Lumley's theatre. She broke the contract by agreeing to perform at a rival theatre for higher fees. Lumley sought both specific performance of the positive obligation to perform for him and an injunction to restrain her from performing elsewhere. The court declined to grant specific performance (courts will not compel personal service). However, an injunction to enforce the negative covenant (not to perform elsewhere) was granted: this did not force performance but merely restrained a breach of a specific promise. If the injunction would have the practical effect of forcing performance (leaving her with no other employment) it would not be granted; on the facts it was not oppressive. Lumley v Wagner is the foundation for using injunctions in entertainment and sports contracts. Important for FE-1 on equitable remedies.

case_name: Co-operative Insurance Society Ltd v Argyll Stores (Holdings) Ltd
legal_principle: Specific performance of a positive obligation to carry on business continuously will not normally be granted, as it requires constant supervision by the court, may cause severe hardship to the defendant, and could compel the defendant to carry on trading at a loss. The general rule against specific performance requiring ongoing court supervision applies unless there are exceptional circumstances.
key_quote: It has been the settled practice of courts … not to make orders which require constant supervision … [specific performance] of a business operation would require such supervision.
full_summary: Argyll Stores operated a supermarket as anchor tenant in the Co-operative's shopping centre under a 35-year lease containing a 'keep open' covenant. Argyll decided to close the supermarket as it was unprofitable. The Co-operative sued for specific performance to compel continued trading. The House of Lords refused specific performance: an order to 'keep open' a business was an order requiring ongoing performance that courts could not practically supervise and which could cause substantial losses to the defendant. The Co-operative's loss was compensable in damages. The policy against ordering specific performance for continuous business obligations is long-standing and well-founded. Co-op v Argyll Stores is the leading modern authority on the limits of specific performance for ongoing business obligations. Critical for FE-1 on equitable remedies.

legal_principle: Damages for breach of contract may include compensation for mental distress, disappointment, and loss of enjoyment where the very object of the contract was to provide pleasure, relaxation, and enjoyment. The court may award damages for non-pecuniary loss where the contract is of a kind whose breach foreseeably causes disappointment or distress.
key_quote: Where the object of the contract is to provide pleasure and enjoyment, damages may be awarded for disappointment and distress when that object is defeated.
full_summary: Jarvis booked a winter ski holiday from Swans Tours based on a brochure describing an excellent chalet with a 'house party' and 'yodeller evening,' professional ski school, and good facilities. The reality was drastically different: few other guests, no English-speaking hosts, a poor-quality party, and inadequate skiing. The Court of Appeal upheld an award for loss of enjoyment and mental distress beyond mere economic loss. Lord Denning held that where the very contract was to provide pleasure and enjoyment, and that promise was broken, damages could include the distress and disappointment caused. Jarvis v Swans Tours is the leading case on damages for non-pecuniary loss in holiday and leisure contracts. It is consistently tested in FE-1 on the measure and heads of contractual damages.

case_name: Herne Bay Steam Boat Co v Hutton
legal_principle: A contract is not frustrated merely because one of the purposes for which it was made has been frustrated, if there remain other purposes for which the contract can still be performed and has value to the other party. The test is whether the frustrating event strikes at the root of the contract as a whole, not merely at one party's particular motive for entering it.
key_quote: The purpose of the hire was not wholly destroyed by the naval review being cancelled … the contract had a further object which could still be fulfilled.
full_summary: Hutton hired the Herne Bay Steam Boat Company's vessel for two days to take passengers to view the naval review at Spithead (as part of King Edward VII's coronation celebrations) and to cruise around the fleet. King Edward fell ill; the coronation and naval review were cancelled. Unlike Krell v Henry (where the entire purpose of the contract was the coronation event), the vessel could still be used for cruising around the fleet even without the review taking place. The Court of Appeal held the contract was not frustrated: while one purpose (viewing the review) was defeated, the vessel could still cruise, and the hiring had commercial value to Hutton even without the royal review. Herne Bay v Hutton is a companion case to Krell v Henry and is crucial for FE-1 in distinguishing frustration of one party's purpose (not sufficient) from frustration of the contract's entire basis.

case_name: Metropolitan Water Board v Dick Kerr & Co Ltd
legal_principle: Where performance of a contract is prohibited by government order during wartime and the interruption is so radical that if resumed it would be, in effect, a different contract from that entered into, the contract is frustrated. The temporary nature of the prohibition is irrelevant if it would, when lifted, require the parties to perform on fundamentally different terms from the original contract.
key_quote: If the interruption is so long and so fundamental that it would change the nature of the obligation, the contract is frustrated.
full_summary: Dick Kerr contracted to construct a reservoir for the Metropolitan Water Board over six years. During World War I, the government ordered the work to stop and directed Dick Kerr's equipment to other war purposes. Dick Kerr argued the contract was frustrated. The House of Lords held the contract was frustrated: the wartime prohibition was not merely a temporary suspension—it was so extensive and so fundamental in its nature and duration that resuming performance after the war would involve an entirely different obligation. The contract effectively ceased to exist. Metropolitan Water Board v Dick Kerr is an important case for FE-1 on frustration by government intervention and the distinction between temporary and permanent interruptions that make performance radically different.

case_name: Tsakiroglou & Co Ltd v Noblee Thorl GmbH
legal_principle: A contract for the sale and shipment of goods is not frustrated merely because the customary or expected route (such as via the Suez Canal) becomes unavailable, if an alternative route remains possible. Mere increase in cost or inconvenience of performance—without making performance radically different in nature—does not frustrate a contract.
key_quote: Increased expense and difficulty of performance, without more, do not frustrate a contract; performance via an alternative route, though more expensive, does not radically change the nature of the obligation.
full_summary: Tsakiroglou agreed to ship groundnuts from Port Sudan to Hamburg. The Suez Canal was closed following the 1956 Suez Crisis. Shipment via the Cape of Good Hope was possible but more expensive and time-consuming. Tsakiroglou argued the contract was frustrated. The House of Lords held the contract was not frustrated: no specific route was stipulated; performance via the Cape was physically possible, albeit more costly. Increased expense or inconvenience does not amount to frustration unless performance would be radically different in character. Contrast Davis Contractors (where prolonged delay made performance fundamentally different). Tsakiroglou is important for FE-1 in establishing that financial hardship or increased cost is not sufficient for frustration.

legal_principle: A governess who has concealed her previous marriage is not required to disclose this fact unless directly asked. A false statement made in response to a direct question is misrepresentation; however, mere concealment without a direct inquiry is not an actionable misrepresentation, as there is generally no duty to volunteer information. The contract was voidable only on proof of active misrepresentation.
key_quote: Concealment of a previous marriage without a direct inquiry is not an actionable misrepresentation; a party is not generally bound to volunteer information.
full_summary: Miss Krell was engaged as a governess and had concealed that she had been previously married (relevant because certain employers required their governesses to be single). When the previous marriage was discovered, the employer sought to rescind the contract. The court held that mere concealment without a direct inquiry was not an actionable misrepresentation. There was no general duty to volunteer information about the previous marriage unless asked. A contract may be avoided for misrepresentation only if a false statement was positively made; silence alone (absent a duty to disclose or a direct question) does not give rise to a misrepresentation claim. Fletcher v Krell illustrates the non-disclosure rule in misrepresentation law (general principle of caveat emptor) and is used in FE-1 to contrast with cases where there is a duty to speak (uberrimae fidei contracts, continuing representations).

legal_principle: A contract made for an immoral purpose is unenforceable, even if the claimant's contribution was not inherently immoral. Where a person knowingly supplies goods or services to another for an immoral purpose in which they participate, the contract is tainted by illegality and the supplier cannot recover the agreed price.
key_quote: A party who knowingly participates in a contract for an immoral purpose cannot enforce it, even if what he supplied was not in itself immoral.
full_summary: Pearce hired out a brougham (a type of carriage) to Brooks, a known prostitute, knowing it was to be used to attract clients. Brooks failed to pay the full hire charge. Pearce sued for the balance. The court held the contract was unenforceable: Pearce had let the carriage knowing it was to be used to facilitate prostitution (an immoral purpose). By knowingly participating in the immoral venture, Pearce could not enforce the contract even though the hire of a carriage was not inherently illegal. Pearce v Brooks is a foundational case on the illegality doctrine in contracts. It illustrates the 'turpitude' principle: knowledge of and participation in an immoral purpose taints the contract as to the knowing party. The contrast is with cases where a supplier is ignorant of the illegal purpose. Important for FE-1 on illegality and public policy.

case_name: Archibolds (Freightage) Ltd v S Spanglett Ltd
legal_principle: A contract for the carriage of goods by a carrier who lacks the appropriate statutory licence is not necessarily unenforceable by the innocent party if that party was unaware of the carrier's illegal status. A contract is unenforceable for illegality only to the extent that the innocent party knew about or participated in the unlawful conduct.
key_quote: Where the contract is not on its face illegal but is performed illegally without the knowledge of one of the parties, that party may enforce the contract.
full_summary: Spanglett carried a consignment of whisky belonging to Archibolds using a van that was not licensed for the carriage of goods owned by others (it was licensed only to carry Spanglett's own goods). The whisky was stolen during transit. Archibolds, who had been unaware of the licensing deficiency, sued for the loss. The Court of Appeal held Archibolds could recover: the contract was not on its face illegal; Spanglett's lack of licence was a matter of its own legal status, and Archibolds was wholly ignorant of it. An innocent party, unaware of the illegal element introduced by the other party's conduct, is not deprived of a right of action. This qualification to the illegality doctrine is important for FE-1: it shows that the doctrine primarily operates against knowing participants.

legal_principle: A person who has killed another in circumstances amounting to manslaughter cannot recover under an indemnity clause in an insurance policy for the civil liability that arises from that killing. Where recovery would be contrary to public policy—because it would effectively allow the wrongdoer to benefit from their serious criminal or quasi-criminal act—the court will not enforce the contractual indemnity.
key_quote: Public policy … prevents a person from recovering under an insurance policy for civil liability resulting from deliberate acts causing death or grievous bodily harm.
full_summary: Barr shot and killed Gray's husband during an altercation (he was acquitted of manslaughter as there was no intent). Gray, as administratrix of her husband's estate, succeeded in a civil action against Barr for damages. Barr sought an indemnity under his insurance policy for the civil damages. The Court of Appeal held the indemnity was unenforceable: public policy prevents a person from receiving insurance indemnity for civil liability arising from a deliberate criminal or seriously blameworthy act causing death. Even though Barr was criminally acquitted, his deliberate act causing death placed the claim squarely within the public policy bar. Gray v Barr is an important case for FE-1 on illegality and public policy in the context of insurance and ex turpi causa.

case_name: Ryan v Mutual Tontine Westminster Chambers Association
legal_principle: Specific performance will not be granted to enforce an obligation to provide a porter who is constantly available, as this would require continuous supervision by the court. Where supervision of performance is practically impossible, the court must limit the innocent party to damages rather than a decree of specific performance.
key_quote: An order for specific performance of a contract to provide a resident porter … would require constant supervision by the court and will not be granted.
full_summary: A tenant in a service flat was entitled under his lease to the constant attendance of a resident porter. The association provided a porter but allowed him to absent himself frequently and to take other employment. The tenant sought specific performance of the obligation. The Court of Appeal refused: performance of the obligation required constant attention and its enforcement would require continuous court supervision. The court was not in a position to superintend the day-to-day attendance of a porter. The remedy was damages. Ryan v Mutual Tontine is an early and important authority on the impossibility of supervising specific performance as a bar to the remedy—a principle later restated in Co-operative Insurance v Argyll Stores. Relevant for FE-1 on the principles governing the discretionary grant of equitable remedies.

case_name: James Elliott Construction Ltd v Irish Asphalt Ltd
legal_principle: Implied terms in Irish contract law include those implied by law (such as in the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980) and those implied in fact. Where construction aggregate is sold for use in buildings, there is an implied condition under the Sale of Goods Act that the goods are fit for purpose and of merchantable quality. Defective aggregate causing structural damage gives rise to liability for breach of these implied terms.
key_quote: An implied condition of fitness for purpose under the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 can be established where a seller knows the purpose for which goods are required.
full_summary: Irish Asphalt supplied aggregate containing excessive pyrite to James Elliott, which was used in the construction of housing. The pyrite caused heave and structural damage. James Elliott sought to recover the cost of remediation. The Supreme Court of Ireland considered the implied conditions of the Sale of Goods Act 1980 (Irish), holding that where a seller knows the purpose for which goods are supplied and the buyer relies on the seller's expertise, conditions of merchantable quality and fitness for purpose are implied. The case addressed complex questions of time-bar under Irish limitation law (Statute of Limitations 1957) in latent defect cases and was significant in the context of Ireland's pyrite remediation crisis. Important for Irish FE-1 on implied terms, sale of goods, and latent defects.

case_name: Noreside Construction Ltd v Irish Asphalt Ltd
legal_principle: A supplier's delivery notes and invoices can incorporate standard terms into a contract if those documents are exchanged in a consistent course of dealing and the terms are brought to the buyer's attention. However, terms not forming part of the contract at the time of contracting are not incorporated by subsequent documentation.
key_quote: Standard terms referred to in delivery notes do not form part of the contract if they were not incorporated at the time the contract was made.
full_summary: Noreside Construction sued Irish Asphalt for supplying defective aggregate (pyrite-contaminated) for use in construction projects. A key issue was whether Irish Asphalt's standard terms (including limitation of liability clauses contained in delivery dockets) had been incorporated into the contracts of sale. The High Court found that terms appearing on delivery dockets were not incorporated into contracts that were formed earlier (often by telephone order) without reference to those terms. The timing rule from Olley v Marlborough Court was applied in the Irish context: terms introduced after contract formation are ineffective. The case is important for Irish FE-1 on incorporation of terms in commercial contracts and on the pyrite litigation generally.

legal_principle: In Ireland, an auctioneer's announcement that goods will be put up for auction constitutes an invitation to treat, not an offer capable of acceptance. Bids at auction are offers, and the fall of the hammer constitutes acceptance. The auctioneer is free to withdraw lots before the hammer falls without breaching any contract.
key_quote: A lot put up for auction is an invitation to treat; the fall of the hammer constitutes acceptance of the highest bid.
full_summary: Thompson was the highest bidder at an auction of goods held by McCoubray. Before the hammer fell, the auctioneer announced the lot was withdrawn. Thompson sued for breach of contract. The Irish Court of Common Pleas held there was no binding contract: an auction announcement is an invitation to treat, not an offer. Bids are offers. A contract is only formed when the auctioneer's hammer falls, accepting the highest bid. The lot was properly withdrawn before acceptance. McCoubray v Thompson is the Irish authority confirming the auction rule parallel to English cases and is relevant for FE-1 in the Irish jurisdiction on offer and acceptance in auction sales.

court: House of Lords (on appeal from Northern Ireland)
legal_principle: A deposit made by a father in joint names of himself and his son creates a trust in favour of the son as a third party, giving the son an enforceable right to the deposit despite not being a party to the banking contract. The son may recover the deposit even though he provided no consideration, because the father's intention was to create a benefit for the son.
key_quote: The son had a vested interest as the beneficiary of the trust and could sue the bank directly despite not being party to the contract of deposit.
full_summary: McEvoy senior deposited money with the Belfast Banking Corporation in the joint names of himself and his son. The bank's formal contract was with the senior McEvoy. When the father died, the bank refused to pay the son, arguing the son was not a party to the banking contract and had given no consideration. The House of Lords held that the deposit created a trust: the father had placed money in the son's name, constituting the son as beneficiary of a trust enforceable against the bank. The son's right to the money arose in equity, not from the banking contract itself—circumventing the privity rule by use of the trust mechanism. McEvoy v Belfast Banking Corporation is a leading privity/trust exception case from Northern Ireland, relevant for FE-1 on privity of contract and its exceptions in the Irish and UK context.

court: Supreme Court of the Irish Free State
legal_principle: An auctioneer who receives a deposit from a successful bidder at auction holds the deposit as stakeholder (or as agent of the vendor), and upon rescission of the contract by the purchaser (for breach by the vendor), the purchaser is entitled to the return of the deposit. An agent who parts with a deposit without authority may be personally liable.
key_quote: An auctioneer holding a deposit from a bidder holds it in a fiduciary capacity; if the contract falls through without the buyer's fault, the deposit must be returned.
full_summary: Nolan was the successful bidder at an auction of land. He paid a deposit to Shiels, the auctioneer. The vendor was unable to complete. Nolan sued to recover the deposit. The Irish Supreme Court held that the deposit held by the auctioneer (acting as agent or stakeholder) must be returned where the contract does not proceed through no fault of the buyer. The auctioneer had no authority to retain the deposit. Nolan v Shiels illustrates principles of agency, auctioneer liability, and the right to recover a deposit in Irish law. It is relevant for FE-1 on deposits in land sale contracts and the obligations of auctioneers acting as agents.

case_name: Quinn v Irish Bank Resolution Corporation Ltd
legal_principle: In Ireland, a bank's unilateral variation of a loan agreement's terms may constitute a breach of contract if the power of variation is not clearly conferred by the contract and the variation is not exercised for a legitimate business reason in good faith. Irish courts may imply a duty of good faith in the exercise of contractual discretions, particularly in banking relationships.
key_quote: A contractual discretion to vary loan terms must be exercised in good faith and not arbitrarily or capriciously; a unilateral variation that is not permitted by the contract terms may give rise to breach.
full_summary: Quinn (formerly a major businessman) challenged IBRC's (formerly Anglo Irish Bank's) management of his loan accounts following the nationalisation of Anglo Irish Bank. The High Court considered whether IBRC had contractual authority to vary terms of Quinn's loan agreements and whether the exercise of various discretionary powers was lawful under the loan contracts. The case raised fundamental questions about the scope of contractual discretion in Irish banking law and the extent to which courts will imply a duty of good faith or rationality into the exercise of contractual powers. It is relevant for FE-1 on the construction of banking contracts, implied duties of good faith, and the remedies available to borrowers in Ireland.

legal_principle: In Ireland, an agreement to pay a percentage of future sales as commission on the sale of a business can be a binding contractual commitment even where the parties have not reduced all the details to writing, provided there is sufficient certainty as to the essential terms. Oral agreements for commission on business sales may be enforced if the essential terms are established.
key_quote: An oral agreement for commission on a future sale … is binding if its essential terms are sufficiently certain.
full_summary: McCormack, a business agent, claimed commission for services rendered in facilitating the sale of McCrystal's business. The commission arrangement had not been fully reduced to writing and disputes arose about the terms. The Irish High Court held that the oral agreement for commission was enforceable: the essential terms—the obligation to pay commission and the rate—were sufficiently certain. The case illustrates Irish contract law principles on the enforceability of oral commission agreements and on certainty of contract in the business context. It is relevant for FE-1 on formation, certainty, and the enforceability of informal commercial arrangements in Ireland.

case_name: The Barge Inn Ltd v Quinn Hospitality Ireland Ltd
legal_principle: Under Irish law, whether pre-contractual letters of intent, heads of agreement, and memoranda of understanding give rise to binding obligations depends on whether the parties objectively intended to be bound. A document labelled as non-binding (e.g., 'subject to contract') will ordinarily not give rise to enforceable obligations unless subsequent conduct suggests an intention to be bound.
key_quote: Whether a letter of intent or heads of agreement is binding depends on the objective intention of the parties, assessed in the context of the whole course of dealings.
full_summary: The Barge Inn and Quinn Hospitality entered into various pre-contractual negotiations involving letters of intent and heads of agreement for the sale of a licensed premises business. Disputes arose as to whether any binding contract had been concluded. The High Court examined the sequence of documents and conduct, applying the objective test of intention. The court held that the documents used 'subject to contract' language or were otherwise not intended to be immediately binding; no binding contract was concluded before formal contracts were executed. The case illustrates Irish courts' application of the 'subject to contract' doctrine and the objective approach to pre-contractual documents in commercial transactions. Important for FE-1 on formation of contracts in complex commercial negotiations.

case_name: AIG Group UK plc v Mark Redler & Co Solicitors
legal_principle: Where a solicitor acts negligently in relation to a mortgage transaction but the lender would have suffered some loss even if the solicitor had acted competently, damages for breach of the solicitor's undertaking are limited to the additional loss caused by the breach. The SAAMCO principle limits damages to the foreseeable loss within the scope of the defendant's duty; solicitors are not automatically liable for the entirety of a lender's mortgage loss if some loss would have occurred regardless.
key_quote: The SAAMCO principle requires that the defendant be liable only for the loss that falls within the scope of his duty … damages are assessed on the difference between the actual position and what it would have been had the breach not occurred.
full_summary: AIG, insuring Mark Redler & Co Solicitors, sought to limit the scope of loss for which the solicitors were liable after negligent handling of a remortgage. The Supreme Court held that a solicitor who breaches an undertaking to a lender is liable only for the loss that flows from that breach within the scope of the duty, not the full mortgage loss if some loss would have occurred in any event. The SAAMCO (South Australia Asset Management Corp v York Montague Ltd [1997] AC 191) principle applies: damages are confined to the difference between the actual position and what would have occurred with proper performance. The case is important in professional negligence and for understanding the measure of damages in solicitors' warranty/undertaking cases with application in the Irish FE-1 curriculum.

case_name: Gray v Hibernian Insurance Co Ltd
court: Supreme Court of the Irish Free State
legal_principle: Under Irish law, an insurance policy taken out to cover criminal or quasi-criminal conduct is contrary to public policy and unenforceable. An insured cannot recover under a policy for losses arising directly from their own criminal act; permitting such recovery would undermine public policy and the general principle ex turpi causa non oritur actio.
key_quote: A contract of insurance cannot be enforced by an insured to recover for losses flowing directly from his own illegal or criminal act.
full_summary: Gray sought to recover under a motor insurance policy for injury and damage caused in an incident connected to his own unlawful conduct. The Irish Supreme Court held the claim was barred by the ex turpi causa principle: no action can arise from the claimant's own wrongdoing. An insurance contract cannot be used to indemnify a party against the direct consequences of their own criminal act — such a policy would be void as contrary to public policy. Gray v Hibernian Insurance is the Irish authority for the public policy bar in insurance law and is important for FE-1 on the interaction of illegality, public policy, and insurance contracts in the Irish context.
